nativeRange.habitat: Shallow rocky shorelines, tide pools, and reef margins down to about 10 m, often sheltering in empty shells, rubble, rock crevices, and dense algal turf.

diet.notes: In the wild, juveniles tend to feed on small invertebrates while adults shift toward a more herbivorous diet dominated by benthic algae. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet of mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped marine meats, and herbivore preparations or dried marine seaweed offered two to three times daily; readily takes prepared dry foods when tank-bred.

avoidWith: other Molly Miller Blennies in undersized tanks
delicate ornamental shrimp (may be picked at)

summary: A hardy, mottled tan-and-black combtooth blenny from the Atlantic and Mediterranean, commonly sold as a captive-bred algae grazer for reef tanks. Generally peaceful, popular for its appetite for nuisance algae and reported willingness to pick at Aiptasia.
careNotes: The Molly Miller Blenny is widely sold as a captive-bred reef-safe utility fish and is commonly considered beginner-friendly. It is generally peaceful with dissimilar tankmates but has been known to behave territorially toward other blennies or fish of similar body shape, so it tends to do best as the only Molly Miller in a tank. Sources broadly agree on a 30 US gal (~114 L) minimum from retailers; given its active perch-and-dart behavior, taste for grazing surfaces, and the prose emphasis on plenty of live rock and hiding places, biasing to a ~120 L footprint is reasonable. Adult size is reported around 10 cm by retailers and up to 12 cm by FishBase wild data — values used here reflect the upper-end spread. Like other blennies it has been reported as a moderate jumper, so a tightly-fitting lid is generally advised. It is widely cited as a useful algae grazer and there are repeated reports of individuals picking at nuisance Aiptasia anemones, though this behavior is anecdotal and not guaranteed. Reef-safety is generally considered good, but the species may occasionally nip at small ornamental shrimp or pick at corals. Lifespan is not stated by LiveAquaria or FishBase; secondary aggregator sources commonly report 7-9 years in well-kept aquaria.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing; eggs are demersal and adhesive, anchored to a substrate via a filamentous adhesive pad with male guarding behavior. Captive breeding is established in tank-bred lines (e.g., Sustainable Aquatics) but is generally considered moderately difficult for hobbyists due to the need to rear pelagic larvae.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Husbandry baseline: 30 US gal minimum, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, peaceful (aggressive only toward similarly shaped fish), reef compatible, omnivore, max 4 in, moderate care, Caribbean origin.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy authority: Blenniidae, Blenniiformes, Salariinae. Max 12.0 cm TL, depth 0-10 m, tropical, marine reef-associated. Distribution: Western Atlantic (Bermuda, Florida, Gulf of Mexico to Brazil), Eastern Atlantic (Mauritania, Canary Islands to South Africa), Mediterranean, Northwest Pacific (Japan, Taiwan). Juveniles feed on invertebrates, adults shift to algae. Oviparous with demersal adhesive eggs.

additional.name: Sustainable Aquatics
additional.notes: Tank-bred source. Confirms 4 in adult size, >30 gal recommended, peaceful temperament, Caribbean origin. Tank-bred individuals are weaned onto captive dry foods (Hatchery Diet), supporting 'easy' feedingDifficulty.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive-bred via paired spawning on cleaned substrate; males guard demersal adhesive eggs. Larval rearing is pelagic and challenging for hobbyists, though commercial captive-bred stock is regularly available.
